questions.prompt: Which expression correctly computes the last digit of a positive int variable n?
questions.explanation: The modulus operator % returns the remainder of integer division. For any positive int, n % 10 is the ones-place digit (e.g., 4729 % 10 is 9). n / 10 removes the last digit instead of returning it.

choices: put
pute
mpu
mput
questions.correctText: put
questions.explanation: substring(start, end) returns characters from index start up to but not including index end. "Computer" indices: C(0) o(1) m(2) p(3) u(4) t(5) e(6) r(7). Indices 3, 4, 5 are 'p', 'u', 't', producing "put".

questions.prompt:
Why is the following comparison considered poor practice for comparing String values for equality?

if (name1 == name2) { ... }
choices: == checks whether the two references point to the same object, not whether the strings contain the same characters.
== always returns false for String variables.
== throws a NullPointerException whenever it is used on a String.
== compares only the first character of each String.
questions.correctText: == checks whether the two references point to the same object, not whether the strings contain the same characters.
questions.explanation: For reference types like String, == tests reference identity (same object in memory). To compare the contents (character sequences), use name1.equals(name2). The == operator may coincidentally return true for string literals due to interning, which makes the bug intermittent.

questions.prompt:
What value is stored in result after this code runs?

double result = Math.pow(2, 5) + Math.sqrt(49);
questions.explanation: Math.pow(2, 5) returns 32.0 (2 raised to the 5th power). Math.sqrt(49) returns 7.0. Their sum is 39.0. Both Math methods return double, so the result is double.

choices: Polymorphism
Method overloading
Unboxing (autoboxing/unboxing)
Type casting via (int)
questions.correctText: Unboxing (autoboxing/unboxing)
questions.explanation: Java automatically converts between primitive types and their wrapper classes. Wrapping an int in an Integer is autoboxing; extracting the int value from an Integer is unboxing. Both happen implicitly in arithmetic expressions and assignments.

questions.prompt: Given int x = 5; int y = 10;, which expression evaluates to true?
questions.explanation: (x > 0) is true. With ||, if the left operand is true, the whole expression is true regardless of the right operand (short-circuit evaluation). The other expressions all evaluate to false because their controlling clauses fail.

questions.prompt: By De Morgan's Laws, the expression !(a && b) is logically equivalent to which of the following?
choices: a || b
questions.explanation: De Morgan's Laws state that the negation of a conjunction is the disjunction of the negations: !(a && b) is equivalent to !a || !b. Likewise, !(a || b) is equivalent to !a && !b.

questions.prompt:
Suppose obj is a reference that might be null. Why does the following safely avoid a NullPointerException?

if (obj != null && obj.getValue() > 0) { ... }
choices: Java automatically catches NullPointerException inside if conditions.
Short-circuit evaluation: && stops evaluating once the left operand is false, so obj.getValue() is never called when obj is null.
The != operator suppresses any later exceptions.
obj.getValue() returns 0 when obj is null.
questions.correctText: Short-circuit evaluation: && stops evaluating once the left operand is false, so obj.getValue() is never called when obj is null.
questions.explanation: Java's && and || are short-circuit operators. When the left operand of && is false, the overall expression must be false, so Java skips the right operand. This idiom (null check then dereference) is the standard way to guard against NullPointerException.

questions.prompt: Which loop will run forever (infinite loop)?
choices: for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) { System.out.println(i); }
int i = 0; while (i < 5) { System.out.println(i); i++; }
int i = 0; while (i < 5) { System.out.println(i); }
int i = 5; while (i > 0) { System.out.println(i); i--; }
questions.correctText: int i = 0; while (i < 5) { System.out.println(i); }
questions.explanation: The loop never modifies i, so the condition i < 5 stays true forever. The fix is to increment i inside the loop. The other choices all reach a terminating condition because their counter is updated each pass.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is a valid use of an enhanced for loop (for-each) to print every element of int[] data?
choices: for (int i = 0; i < data.length; i++) { System.out.println(data); }
for (int value : data) { System.out.println(value); }
for (int value = 0; value : data; value++) { System.out.println(value); }
foreach (int value in data) { System.out.println(value); }
questions.correctText: for (int value : data) { System.out.println(value); }
questions.explanation: Java's enhanced for syntax is for (Type variable : iterable) { ... }. It assigns each element of data to value in turn. The keyword is for, not foreach, and the separator is a colon, not in (which is Python/C# syntax).

questions.prompt: What is the primary purpose of a constructor in a Java class?
choices: To free memory when an object is no longer used.
To initialize the state (instance variables) of a newly created object.
To return a value to the calling method.
To override methods inherited from a parent class.
questions.correctText: To initialize the state (instance variables) of a newly created object.
questions.explanation: A constructor runs automatically when an object is created with new. Its job is to assign starting values to the object's instance variables. Constructors have the same name as the class and no return type (not even void). Memory cleanup is handled by Java's garbage collector, not by constructors.

questions.prompt: Which keyword distinguishes a class-level member shared by every instance from a per-instance member?
choices: final
private
static
this
questions.correctText: static
questions.explanation: A static field or method belongs to the class itself, not to any one object. All instances share the same static variable, and static methods can be called via ClassName.methodName(). Instance members exist separately inside each object.

questions.prompt: Why are instance variables typically declared private and accessed through public getter and setter methods?
choices: To make the class run faster at runtime.
To support encapsulation: hiding internal state and controlling how it can be read or modified.
Because the Java compiler will not compile public fields.
To allow other classes to extend the class without using extends.
questions.correctText: To support encapsulation: hiding internal state and controlling how it can be read or modified.
questions.explanation: Encapsulation is a core OOP principle: keep internal data private and expose only a controlled public interface. This lets you validate inputs in setters, change the internal representation later without breaking client code, and protect invariants. Public fields are legal Java but break encapsulation.

choices: It refers to the current object, distinguishing the instance variable name from the local parameter name.
It calls the superclass constructor.
It marks name and age as static.
It creates a new Dog object.
questions.correctText: It refers to the current object, distinguishing the instance variable name from the local parameter name.
questions.explanation: When a parameter has the same identifier as an instance variable, the parameter shadows the field inside the method. Writing this.name explicitly refers to the instance variable of the current object so you can assign the parameter value to it. super(...) is what calls the superclass constructor.

questions.prompt: Which method below is correctly designed as an accessor (getter) for a private int field score?
choices: public void getScore() { return score; }
public int getScore() { return score; }
private int getScore() { return score; }
public int getScore(int score) { return this.score; }
questions.correctText: public int getScore() { return score; }
questions.explanation: An accessor must be public so callers can use it, must declare a return type matching the field (int, not void), takes no parameters, and returns the field's current value. The void version cannot return a value; the private version would not be usable from outside the class.

questions.prompt: Which statement correctly declares an int array containing the values 4, 8, 15, 16, 23, 42?
questions.explanation: Java's array initializer literal uses curly braces. The size is inferred from the number of values. Other syntaxes shown borrow from C++ (fixed size with [6]), Python (parentheses), or generic collection notation (Array<int>), none of which compile here.

questions.prompt: Given int[] arr = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50};, what does the expression arr.length - 1 represent?
choices: The last value stored in the array (50).
The number of valid index positions.
The index of the final element.
An ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException.
questions.correctText: The index of the final element.
questions.explanation: Java array indices run from 0 to length - 1. For this array of length 5, the last valid index is 4, accessed as arr[arr.length - 1] (which is 50). The length field gives the count of elements, not an index.

questions.prompt: When a method takes an int[] as a parameter, what is passed in Java?
choices: A deep copy of every element in the array.
A copy of the reference (address), so changes to elements inside the method are visible to the caller.
Only the first element of the array.
The array's length, but not the values.
questions.correctText: A copy of the reference (address), so changes to elements inside the method are visible to the caller.
questions.explanation: Java is pass-by-value, but for reference types the value being passed is the reference. The method gets a separate variable pointing at the same underlying array, so reassigning the parameter does not affect the caller, but modifying elements (e.g., a[0] = 99) does.

choices: [A, X, B, C]
[A, B, X, C]
[X, A, B, C]
[A, B, C, X]
questions.correctText: [A, X, B, C]
questions.explanation: list.add(index, element) inserts element at the given index and shifts existing elements right by one. Starting from [A, B, C], inserting "X" at index 1 gives [A, X, B, C].

questions.prompt: Why does removing elements from an ArrayList during a standard indexed for loop frequently cause bugs?
choices: Java does not permit removal from an ArrayList under any circumstances.
The list throws a NullPointerException whenever remove is called.
After remove(i), elements shift left, so the index i now refers to a different element and the next element can be skipped.
remove always deletes the first element regardless of the argument.
questions.correctText: After remove(i), elements shift left, so the index i now refers to a different element and the next element can be skipped.
questions.explanation: When you remove an element at index i, every element after it shifts down by one and the list's size shrinks by one. If you then increment i, you jump over the new element at position i. Common fixes: iterate backward (size - 1 down to 0), or do not increment i when an element is removed.

questions.prompt: Why is ArrayList<int> not a valid declaration in Java?
choices: Generic type parameters must be reference types; primitive types must be wrapped (e.g., ArrayList<Integer>).
ArrayList may not store numerical values.
Only ArrayList<String> is supported in AP CS A.
ArrayList requires the diamond operator <>.
questions.correctText: Generic type parameters must be reference types; primitive types must be wrapped (e.g., ArrayList<Integer>).
questions.explanation: Java generics work only with reference types. To store int values, use ArrayList<Integer>; autoboxing converts int values to Integer objects on insertion and unboxes them on retrieval. Other primitives use their wrapper classes (Double, Boolean, Character, etc.).

questions.prompt: Given int[][] grid = new int[4][6];, how many rows and columns does grid have?
choices: 6 rows, 4 columns
4 rows, 6 columns
24 rows, 1 column
It is a 1-D array of length 10.
questions.correctText: 4 rows, 6 columns
questions.explanation: In Java, the first dimension is the number of rows and the second is the number of columns. new int[4][6] allocates 4 row arrays, each of length 6, for a total of 24 int slots all initialized to 0.

questions.prompt: Which loop structure performs a standard row-major traversal of int[][] grid, visiting every element?
questions.explanation: Row-major traversal walks each row completely before moving on. The outer loop runs over rows (grid.length), the inner loop runs over columns within that row (grid[r].length). The other options visit only rows as a whole, mix up row and column counts, or treat grid as if it were 1-D.

questions.prompt: Which keyword indicates that one class inherits the public and protected members of another?
choices: implements
extends
inherits
super
questions.correctText: extends
questions.explanation: Java uses class Sub extends Super to establish single inheritance from a class. The keyword implements is used for interfaces. super is used inside a subclass to call the parent constructor or a parent method, not to declare inheritance.

questions.prompt: Inside a subclass constructor, what is the purpose of the statement super(name);?
choices: It declares the subclass to be abstract.
It calls the superclass constructor that takes a single matching argument, so the inherited state is initialized first.
It overrides the superclass constructor.
It creates a new object of the superclass type.
questions.correctText: It calls the superclass constructor that takes a single matching argument, so the inherited state is initialized first.
questions.explanation: super(...) invokes the parent constructor whose parameter list matches the arguments. It must appear as the first statement of the subclass constructor. This ensures inherited instance variables are initialized before the subclass adds its own state.

choices: "generic noise" — Java uses the declared type Animal.
"woof" — polymorphism dispatches to the actual object's overridden method at runtime.
Nothing — the call fails to compile.
Both "generic noise" and "woof" print.
questions.correctText: "woof" — polymorphism dispatches to the actual object's overridden method at runtime.
questions.explanation: This is dynamic dispatch (polymorphism). The compiler verifies the call against the declared type (Animal must have speak()), but at runtime Java executes the version belonging to the actual object, which is a Dog. So a.speak() prints "woof".

questions.prompt: What is a defining property of an abstract class in Java?
choices: It is exactly the same as an interface and cannot have any methods with bodies.
It cannot be instantiated directly with new and can contain a mix of implemented and abstract methods that subclasses must override.
Every method must be declared static.
It is automatically marked final and cannot be extended.
questions.correctText: It cannot be instantiated directly with new and can contain a mix of implemented and abstract methods that subclasses must override.
questions.explanation: Abstract classes are designed to be extended. They may declare abstract methods (no body) that concrete subclasses must implement, and they may also include concrete methods and instance variables that subclasses inherit. You cannot write new AbstractClass(...), only new ConcreteSubclass(...).

questions.prompt: Why is it considered good practice to override the toString() method (inherited from Object) in your own classes?
choices: Java will not compile a class that does not override toString().
It provides a meaningful String representation of the object for printing and debugging instead of the default ClassName@hashcode output.
It removes the need to write a constructor.
It automatically makes the class implement equals() correctly.
questions.correctText: It provides a meaningful String representation of the object for printing and debugging instead of the default ClassName@hashcode output.
questions.explanation: Object.toString() returns something like "Dog@1540e19d", which is rarely helpful. Overriding it lets System.out.println(obj) and string concatenation produce readable text such as "Dog[name=Rex, age=4]". Overriding toString() does not affect equals() or hashCode().

questions.prompt: What is the role of the base case in a recursive method?
choices: It is the initial call from main that starts the recursion.
It is the condition that stops further recursive calls and returns a known result, preventing infinite recursion.
It always doubles the input before recursing.
It is an optional optimization rarely needed in AP CS A code.
questions.correctText: It is the condition that stops further recursive calls and returns a known result, preventing infinite recursion.
questions.explanation: Every correct recursive method has at least one base case that returns directly without calling itself. Without one, recursive calls continue until the call stack overflows (StackOverflowError). The recursive case must also progress toward the base case on each call.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best describes binary search on a sorted array as a recursive algorithm?
choices: Compare the target to every element from index 0 to length - 1.
Compare the target to the middle element; if not equal, recurse on the appropriate half (left if target is smaller, right if larger). Base case: empty range means not found.
Sort the array first, then return the first element greater than the target.
Visit each element of the array exactly twice.
questions.correctText: Compare the target to the middle element; if not equal, recurse on the appropriate half (left if target is smaller, right if larger). Base case: empty range means not found.
questions.explanation: Binary search divides the search space in half each step, giving O(log n) running time on a sorted array. The recursive structure has two base cases (range exhausted means not found; middle element equals target means found) and one recursive case that narrows to either the lower or upper half.
